flags: The description says the top overhangs the frame, but the top is only 18-1/2" wide (11-1/4" 1x12 plus 7-1/4" 1x8 trim) and the leg-to-leg depth is also 18-1/2" (3/4" front leg + 17" frame + 3/4" back leg). So the top is FLUSH front and back; it overhangs only lengthwise, 3/4" at each end (71" top over a 69-1/2" frame). With the 3/8" back panel applied outside the back legs the assembly is 18-7/8" deep, so the top actually stops 3/8" short at the back. The prose states this rather than repeating 'an even overhang on all four sides'.

The 69-1/2" bottom tray liner equals the frame's full OUTER length (66-1/2" long rail plus 1-1/2" of short rail at each end), so it lies ON TOP of the bottom frame — it cannot drop inside, because the inside opening is only 66-1/2" x 10". It is also 16-1/2" against the frame's 17" depth, so it is 1/2" narrow; the source does not say where that 1/2" goes.

Which frame member runs full length is not printed by the box-geometry solver for this plan. The reading used in the prose (17" short rails full depth, 66-1/2" long rails between) is proved by arithmetic on the plan's own data: 66-1/2 + 1-1/2 + 1-1/2 = 69-1/2", which is exactly the tray liner length and 3/4" shy of the 71" top and back panel at each end.

The vertical position of the two frames on the 31-3/4" legs is NOT specified. The 24-3/4" clear tray opening quoted in the prose is DERIVED from both frames sitting flush with the leg ends (31-3/4 less 3-1/2 of 2x4 at top and bottom).
The back panel is 31-1/2" tall against 31-3/4" legs — 1/4" short. Not resolved by the source.

Lumber verified sufficient with a 1/8" kerf: the 2x4 parts pair 66-1/2" + 17" on each of four 8 ft boards, and six 31-3/4" legs give three per 8 ft 1x4 across two boards. The 71" top board and 71" trim each come out of their 6 ft stock. No shortfall.

ops.body: Each frame is a rectangle of two 66-1/2" long rails and two 17" short rails, and there are two identical frames. The 17" short rails run the full depth at each end with the long rails fitting between them, which makes each frame 69-1/2" long overall and 17" deep, with a clear opening of 66-1/2" x 10" inside — that 69-1/2" is exactly the length of the plywood tray liner, which is how the assembly reads. Drive two 3" self-tapping screws through the face of each short rail into the end grain of the long rail, working on a flat floor so nothing builds in a twist. Measure both diagonals of each frame and adjust until they match before the last screws go tight, then set the two frames side by side and confirm they are the same size as each other.

The tray liner is 69-1/2" x 16-1/2", and 69-1/2" is the frame's full outside length — so it lies flat ON TOP of the bottom frame rather than dropping inside it, since the inside opening is only 66-1/2" x 10". Run a bead of glue along every rail, lay the liner down, and fix it with 3/4" brad nails into all four rails, keeping the nails back from the plywood edges so they don't blow out through the 3/8" edge. The liner is 16-1/2" against the frame's 17" depth, so you have 1/2" to place: set it flush at the back where the panel will close it off and let the 1/2" show at the front. The whole point of the liner is that bark and debris land on plywood rather than dropping between the rails, so seal the joint at the perimeter with glue as you go.

All six legs are 31-3/4" 1x4. The four front legs go on in pairs — two at each end on the FRONT face of the two frames — and the two back legs go one at each end on the BACK face, which is what makes the front read as a paired A-frame and the back a single flat leg. Because the legs land on the outside faces rather than between the rails, the finished depth is 3/4" + 17" + 3/4" = 18-1/2", exactly the combined width of the 11-1/4" top board and the 7-1/4" edge trim that go on later. Clamp the top frame flush with the leg tops and the bottom frame flush with the leg bottoms, keep every outside edge flush, and check the piece stands square before fastening with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails driven through the 3/4" leg into the 2x4 rails. Set that way the clear firewood opening between the two frames works out at 24-3/4"; the source does not state the frame heights, so if you want a different tray opening, decide it now — everything above depends on it.

The back panel is 31-1/2" x 71" of 3/8" plywood and it goes on the OUTSIDE of the two back legs, closing the back of the piece completely — this is the part that turns an open firewood cradle into a console. At 71" it is 3/4" longer than the 69-1/2" frames at each end, and it sets the same 3/4" overhang the 71" top will have, so align it by its ends rather than by the legs. It is 31-1/2" tall against 31-3/4" legs; line the panel up with the leg tops and let the 1/4" difference fall at the floor where it will not show. Glue and 3/4" brads into both back legs and into the back rail of each frame, working from the middle outward so the panel doesn't bow.

The top is two boards laid side by side: the 11-1/4" wide 1x12 top board and the 7-1/4" wide 1x8 edge trim, both 71" long, making an 18-1/2" wide top. Glue the two long edges together and clamp them flat, then nail the assembly down into the top frame rails with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. Lengthwise the 71" top overhangs the 69-1/2" frame by 3/4" at each end, flush with the back panel; front to back the 18-1/2" top exactly matches the 18-1/2" leg-to-leg depth, so it finishes flush at the front and, because the 3/8" back panel sits outside the back legs, stops 3/8" shy at the back — set it flush at the front and let that 3/8" land behind. Additional supports or a hinged lift-top compartment can be built into the top if you want hidden storage, but the base build is the plain fixed top described here.

Fill every brad hole, then sand the whole piece — start coarse on the 2x4 frames, which come rough from the mill, and finish fine on the top where hands land. Ease the top's edges and both end corners with the sander so nobody catches a shin on them, and knock the sharp arris off the tray liner's edges too. Apply paint or stain to every face including the inside of the tray, since that surface takes the abuse from bark and split ends. Let the finish cure fully before you load any wood into it.

baseStepTitles: Set the seat height and supports
Add the side pieces last and finish
flags: CORRECTED: the retained cut step said the 1x3 parts come to 'eight 8 ft 1x3'. Seven is right and seven is what the materials list declares. The 35 1x3 pieces total 644-3/4" and pack into seven 96" boards with a 1/8" kerf, but only by cross-pairing different lengths on the same board; a worked seven-board layout is now written into the step.

Two 31-1/2" 1x3 long rails are cut and NO step installs them. They are the same between-the-sides length as the 31-1/2" 1x2 seat supports, but the source never says where they go. The rewrite says so rather than inventing a home for them.

The 1x2 back support is 36-1/2" — 3-1/2" longer than the bench's 33" outside width, so it cannot sit inside the frame the way the description implies. It has to run past the sides. The rewrite states the contradiction and does not resolve it.
Slat gaps of about 3/8" are DERIVED, not authored: eleven 2-1/2" slats total 27-1/2" across the 31-1/2" clear span, leaving 4" over ten gaps.
The 33"-full / 31-1/2"-between reading (31-1/2 + 3/4 + 3/4 = 33) is arithmetic on the plan's own cut list; the box-geometry solver returns nothing for this plan.
The exact position of the four 18-1/4" uprights, the arm assembly, and the seat depth are not specified in the source; the rewrite gives the lengths and stops there.
1x2 verified sufficient: 197" of parts into three 8 ft boards.

ops.body: Cut four 1x3 uprights at 18-1/4", eleven 1x3 back slats at 19", two 1x3 arm parts at 15-3/4", two 1x3 long rails at 31-1/2", three 1x3 seat front/back at 33", eleven 1x3 seat slats at 12-1/4" and two 1x3 side pieces at 17-1/4" from the 1x3. Cut two 1x2 top rails at 33", one 1x2 back support at 36-1/2", two 1x2 seat supports at 31-1/2" and two 1x2 side supports at 15-3/4" from the 1x2. That is 35 pieces of 1x3 totalling 644-3/4" and seven pieces of 1x2 totalling 197", which fit the seven 8 ft 1x3 and three 8 ft 1x2 this plan lists — but only if you mix different lengths on the same board instead of cutting one length at a time. A layout that closes with a 1/8" kerf is: two boards of 33 + 31-1/2 + 18-1/4 + 12-1/4, two boards of five 19s each, one board of 19 + 33 + 18-1/4 + 18-1/4, one board of 17-1/4 + 17-1/4 + 15-3/4 + 15-3/4 + 12-1/4 + 12-1/4, and a last board of seven 12-1/4s. Set a stop block on the miter saw for each repeated length, square every cut, and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

The bench is 33" wide overall: the 33" pieces run the full width and the 31-1/2" pieces fit between the 3/4" side assemblies, since 31-1/2" plus 3/4" at each end comes to 33". Fix the two 31-1/2" 1x2 seat supports between the sides with their top edges 11-1/4" off the ground — the 3/4" seat slats that land on them bring the finished seat to the intended 12" height. Add the extra 1x2 across the back of the middle support for the backrest to lean against; it makes assembly much easier and gives the back something solid to bear on instead of relying on the slat nails alone. Glue every joint and drive two 1-1/4" brads at each end, checking the two supports are level with each other before you commit — a seat support that is out of level shows up in every slat you lay on it.

Put the back-support 1x2 on the inside face of the armrests rather than at the very back — it sits the seat back up a little straighter and looks better than mounting it flush to the rear. Note before you fit it that this piece is cut at 36-1/2", which is 3-1/2" longer than the bench's 33" outside width, so it cannot sit between the sides the way the other 1x2 supports do; it has to run across and past them, or be trimmed to suit. The plan does not settle which, so hold it in place against your assembled sides and decide before nailing. Glue and 1-1/4" brads once you have it where you want it.

The top support is one of the two 33" 1x2 top rails, so like the 33" seat front/back it runs the full outside width and its ends land on the outer faces of the uprights. Set it about 2" down from the top of the 18-1/4" uprights and mark that 2" on both uprights before you offer the rail up, so it goes on level rather than by eye. Glue and two 1-1/4" brads into each upright. On a bench this size you can skip a separate middle support — it holds up fine without one.

Lay the eleven 12-1/4" seat slats across the seat supports front to back, and stand the eleven 19" back slats up against the back supports. Eleven 2-1/2" slats come to 27-1/2" across the 31-1/2" clear span, which leaves 4" of gap spread over ten gaps — about 3/8" each — so cut a scrap spacer to that width and use it between every slat rather than eyeballing them; the eye catches an uneven gap instantly on a slatted piece. Glue each slat and drive two 1-1/4" brads into every support it crosses, keeping the outer slats flush with the frame at each end so the gaps stay symmetrical. One thing the source leaves open: the two 31-1/2" 1x3 long rails are on the cut list but no step installs them, so set them aside for now rather than guessing at a place for them.

Add the 17-1/4" 1x3 side pieces and the 15-3/4" 1x2 side supports last, after the back is on and in place — they tend to overlap the parts already fitted, so leaving them until the end lets you scribe and trim them to the real opening instead of a drawing. Fit the two 15-3/4" 1x3 arm parts the same way, holding each one in place and marking it rather than measuring twice. Glue and 1-1/4" brads throughout. Then fill every nail hole and sand the whole bench — edges, corners, slat ends and the undersides of the arms, because small hands find every splinter — and finish or paint in a child-safe coating, letting it cure fully before the bench goes into use.

baseStepTitles: Frame and clad the two arms
Build the two seat frames — make them identical
Build and panel the back frame
Join the back to the arms
Install the angled back boards and trim
Build the four drawers and hang them on slides
Add seat slats, the pipe rail, and the cushion
flags: REAL SHORTFALL, 1x8: sixteen 24" arm-cladding pieces and fourteen 12-3/4" angled back boards need SEVEN 8 ft 1x8; the materials list declares six. Proof that six cannot work: four 24" pieces need 96" plus three kerfs, so a 96" board holds at most three of them — sixteen 24s therefore need six boards on their own, and those six boards have room for only ten of the fourteen 12-3/4" pieces. The seventh board is not optional.

MISSING ASSEMBLY STEP (the biggest defect): no step installed the two seat frames into the carcass. They were built in step 3 and then never joined to anything, yet step 7 sizes the drawers to 'the openings' and step 8 lays slats across 'the seat frame'. The rewrite of 'Join the back to the arms' now installs them.

The source does not specify how the two seat frames locate in the carcass — their height off the floor, their spacing front to back, or how the ten 9" cross members divide the four drawer bays. The rewrite says to set them level and parallel and to mark both from a single layout, and stops there. Do not read the 26" slide length as a stated frame spacing; it is not.

Drawer faces are 25-5/8" and 25-1/8" against drawer boxes that are 26" across the fronts/backs, so the faces are NARROWER than the boxes they cover. A face cannot overlay an opening it is smaller than. The plan does not say whether the boxes or the faces are the error; the rewrite tells the builder to size the faces to the finished openings and check before drilling.

Already flagged by the existing cut step and left in place: the 102-1/2" seat/back rails and 102" front trim and 102-1/2" top trim cannot come off the 8 ft 2x4, 1x6 and 1x3 the materials list declares. The 9 ft studs cover the 2x4 rails; the 1x6 front trim and 1x3 top trim still need longer stock.

Box geometry (solver, do not contradict): the 102-1/2" back frame long rails run FULL and the 21" studs sit BETWEEN them, proved by the 24" x 102-1/2" back panel. The back frame is therefore 24" tall overall.

Verified sufficient, no shortfall: six 9 ft studs for the six 102-1/2" rails; four 8 ft 2x4 for the arm and cross-member parts; nine 8 ft 1x6 against seven needed (excluding the impossible 102" front trim); one 10 ft 1x10 for all four drawer faces.

ops.body: Cut four arm frame verticals at 24", four arm frame long members at 31-1/2", ten seat frame cross members at 9", four seat frame long rails at 102-1/2", two back frame long rails at 102-1/2" and three back frame studs at 21" from the 2x4. Cut 16 arm end cladding at 24" and 14 angled back boards at 12-3/4" from the 1x8. Cut four arm cladding 1x6 at 24", two arm top 1x6 at 36", one front trim at 102", eight drawer sides at 23-3/4" and eight drawer fronts/backs at 26" from the 1x6. Cut one back panel at 102-1/2" from the 1/4 in plywood. Cut one back retaining strip at 102-1/2" from the 1x2. Cut two top trim at 102-1/2" from the 1x3. Cut four drawer bottoms at 26" from the 1/4 in plywood. Cut two drawer faces (wide) at 25-5/8" and two drawer faces (narrow) at 25-1/8" from the 1x10. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to seven 8 ft 1x8, one 10 ft 1x2 and one 10 ft 1x10. Buy the seventh 1x8 before you start: the materials list only calls for six, and six will not do it — a 96" board holds at most three of the 24" cladding pieces once you allow for the kerf, so the sixteen 24s consume six boards by themselves and leave room for only ten of the fourteen 12-3/4" back boards. Check your lumber before cutting: the longest 2x4 piece is 102-1/2", which will not come off the 8 ft length this plan lists, the longest 1x6 piece is 102", which will not come off the 8 ft length this plan lists and the longest 1x3 piece is 102-1/2", which will not come off the 8 ft length this plan lists — buy a longer length or adjust the cut list to suit. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

ops.title: Join the back and the seat frames to the arms
ops.body:
Stand the two arms up and attach the back frame between them with the 2" screws, driving from the inside where the heads won't show. The back frame is 24" across overall — three 21" studs between the 102-1/2" rails, with 1-1/2" of rail beyond each stud — so that 24" is the figure to work from, and the 102-1/2" rails set the clear distance between the two arms.

Now install the two seat frames, which is what the drawers hang from — they must be joined into the carcass here, not left standing on the floor. Mark their positions on both arms from one layout rather than measuring each arm separately, screw them to the arm frames and to the back frame with the 2" screws, and run a level along the full 102-1/2" length of each before anything goes tight. The two frames must stay level and parallel to each other; the plan does not state their height or spacing, so whatever you choose, transfer the same marks to both ends. If the back is exposed to the room, add the extra 1x cladding boards now with glue and 1-1/4" brads, then check the whole carcass sits flat and square before the drawers go in.

Build four drawer boxes from the 23-3/4" 1x6 sides and the 26" 1x6 fronts and backs, with a 1/4" plywood bottom on each. Measure your actual openings before you assemble anything — on a rough-framed carcass the four bays will not be identical, and a box built to a drawing rather than to its own opening is the usual reason a drawer binds. Mount the 26" slides so each drawer front sits flush with the front edge of the 2x4 frame; if a slide won't reach because the opening is a touch wide, block inward with scrap wood, which is normal and expected here.

The 1x10 drawer faces are listed at 25-5/8" and 25-1/8", which is narrower than the 26" boxes they cover — a face cannot overlay an opening it is smaller than, so hold each face up to its finished opening and cut it to suit rather than trusting those two numbers. Attach the faces from inside with screws and glue, predrilling so the 1x10 doesn't split, and adjust each one for an even gap before the screws go tight.

baseStepTitles: Build a deck module with railing
flags: Box geometry (solver, obeyed in BOTH places): the 7-3/4" members run the FULL length and the 14" members sit BETWEEN them, proved by the 7-3/4" x 15-1/2" room walls and deck floor. This settles the deck railing AND the roof frame identically — the roof's outside footprint is 15-1/2" x 7-3/4", the same as the module below it, which is exactly what makes the system stack. Do not treat the roof as a different size.

Unused / unassigned parts: the cut list has four 7-3/4" room frame pieces, but one room box needs two of them between its two 15-1/2" plywood panels. It also has four 7-3/4" deck railing short sides against only two 14" long sides — one rectangle uses two of each, so two short sides are left over (they are the right length to serve as the roof frame's end members, but the source does not say so).
The roof frame has four 14" long sides. Two of them form the base rectangle with a pair of 7-3/4" end members; where the other two go is not specified in the source, and the rewrite does not guess.

Derived, not authored: with the dowel holes drilled 1/4" deep in each rail as the source instructs, a 2-1/2" spindle leaves a 2" clear rise between rails — which matches the 2" corner posts. The rewrite uses that as a check, not as a new dimension.
The number of spindles per railing side is not specified; twelve are cut for four sides.
Moulding verified sufficient: all the moulding parts total 148-1/2" against the 15 ft (180") declared.

ops.body: A room module is a plywood box on a 1x frame: the two 15-1/2" x 7-3/4" plywood panels are its floor and ceiling, and two of the 7-3/4" 1x4 frame pieces stand between them at the ends. The 7-3/4" pieces run the full depth and the panels close over them, which is what fixes every module at the same 15-1/2" x 15-1/2" footprint that the deck and roof share — get this wrong on one module and nothing stacks. Paint the 1x pieces before assembly wherever you can; it is far easier to get clean coverage on flat stock than to reach a brush inside a finished room. Glue and 1-1/4" brads or screws through the plywood into the frame pieces, checking the box for square across its diagonals before the glue grabs. The cut list gives four frame pieces where one room needs two, so the spares carry over to your next module.

The deck is the 15-1/2" x 7-3/4" plywood floor with a moulding railing built around it. Lay the railing out the same way the modules are laid out: two 7-3/4" short sides run the full depth at the ends and the two 14" long sides fit between them, which gives a rail rectangle 15-1/2" long overall — flush with the deck floor beneath it. Drill the 1/4" dowel holes 1/4" deep in the rails; with a 1/4" socket at each end, the 2-1/2" spindles leave 2" of clear rise between the rails, which is why the corner posts are cut at 2". Glue the spindles into their holes, glue and nail the rail frame together with the 2" corner posts at the four corners, then fix the whole railing down to the plywood floor with 1-1/4" brad nails. Two of the four 7-3/4" short sides are used here; keep the other pair with your moulding offcuts.

The roof frame's base rectangle is built exactly like the deck railing and to exactly the same size: two 14" long sides fitted between a pair of 7-3/4" end members, giving a 15-1/2" outside length that matches the module underneath. That match is the whole point of the roof module — a roof frame that is 1-1/2" off in either direction will rock or gap on top of a room. Cut the gable pieces' 45° ends on the miter saw (the 6-3/4" gable longs and the 6" gable shorts are longest-point measurements) and bring them together at the peak, then cap the ridge with the two 11" 1x2 peak trims, whose ends are cut to a point at 45°. Glue and 1-1/4" brads throughout, and before the glue sets, set the whole roof assembly down on an actual room module and confirm it is square against the room's real footprint — a roof that is square only in isolation can still rock once stacked. The source does not say where the remaining two 14" long sides go, so dry-fit them against your assembly and decide rather than nailing on a guess.

Stack your room, deck, and roof modules in any combination — every module is built to the same 15-1/2" x 7-3/4" footprint, which is what lets them mix and match. Set each new module down dry and check it sits flat on the one below before you commit to a finish; a module that gaps or rocks is telling you its frame is out of square, and that is far easier to correct now than after paint. Sand every edge and corner that a child will handle, and paint or finish each module separately so you can add to the set later without masking off what you already built. Add furnishings and details as separate small builds once the core modules are done.

baseStepTitles: Build and set the gable roof frame
flags: REAL SHORTFALL, 2x4: 21 boards are needed against 12 declared. Proof is not a packing estimate — every single 2x4 part is 70-1/2" or longer (sixteen at 89", two at 77-1/2", three at 70-1/2"), and no two of those fit on one 96" board, so each piece takes its own board.
REAL SHORTFALL, 2x8: four boards needed against 2 declared. All four 2x8 parts are 89" and none pair on a 96" board.

REAL SHORTFALL, 2x6 (the one that is easiest to miss): thirteen 5-1/2" decking boards plus twelve 1/2" gaps cover 77-1/2", but every member of the floor frame is 89" long. That is 11-1/2" of floor left bare, needing two more 2x6 — and all sixteen declared 2x6 are already consumed by the thirteen decking boards and the three roof cross braces. Eighteen are needed.
The 4x4 count is CORRECT at six and is NOT flagged: the two 93" posts take a board each, and a 58-1/4" and a 37-1/2" gable rafter pair on a 96" board with 1/8" to spare, four times over.

The cut list gives TWO corner posts, but the plan supplies four Simpson Strong-Tie post connectors and the description speaks of posts at the corners. The rewrite says the post count does not reconcile and does not invent the missing two.

The floor frame does not close as drawn: all twelve floor members — two outer 2x4, seven joists, two 2x8 rim boards — are 89" long. Members meeting at a corner cannot all be the same length unless they lap; the source does not say which run through. The rewrite tells the builder to settle it on the ground.
Derived: the 96" decking boards over an 89" floor overhang about 3-1/2" at each end.

Post notch dimensions, rafter spacing, and the depth of the lapped 4x4 peak joint are all 'as shown in the reference build' in the source and are not given as numbers. The rewrite describes the technique and does not supply numbers the plan does not contain.
This is a permanent structure: footing sizing, snow and wind loading, and permitting are named in the description and are the builder's local-code responsibility, not something this plan settles.

ops.body:
Cut two floor frame, outer at 89", seven floor joists at 89", two header beams at 77-1/2", three cross beams at 70-1/2" and seven rafters at 89" from the 2x4, 8 ft, ground-contact rated. Cut two floor rim boards at 89" and two ridge beams at 89" from the 2x8, 8 ft, ground-contact rated. Cut two corner posts at 93", four gable rafters, short side at 37-1/2" and four gable rafters, long side at 58-1/4" from the 4x4, 8 ft, ground-contact rated. Cut 13 decking boards at 96" and three roof cross braces at 70-1/2" from the 2x6, 8 ft, ground-contact rated. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

Reconcile the lumber before you buy, because three of the four bills are short. The twenty-one 2x4 parts are every one of them 70-1/2" or longer, so no two share a 96" board and you need 21 boards, not the 12 listed. All four 2x8 parts are 89", so you need 4, not 2. The 2x6 is the subtle one: 13 decking boards at 5-1/2" with twelve 1/2" gaps only cover 77-1/2" of an 89" floor, so you need two more decking boards — 18 2x6 in total, not 16. The 4x4 bill is correct at six: each 93" post takes its own board, and a 58-1/4" and a 37-1/2" gable rafter pair on each of the remaining four with 1/8" to spare, so cut those in pairs and cut them carefully. Note too that the cut list gives two corner posts while the hardware bill gives four post connectors; decide how many posts your structure needs before you cut the 4x4.

Build the floor flat on the ground before it ever goes near the piers — this is the deliberate sequencing choice the whole build rests on, because levelling a bare deck is straightforward and levelling a fully framed structure afterwards is not. Assemble the two 2x8 rim boards and the two 2x4 outer members into the perimeter with 3" exterior screws, then hang the seven 89" joists inside it using pocket holes and the 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws. Note as you lay it out that every floor member is cut at 89", including the perimeter ones — pieces meeting at a corner cannot all be 89" unless some run past and lap the others, so decide on the ground which members run through and adjust the rest to suit before you drive a screw. Measure both diagonals and rack the frame until they match, then set it on the prepared pier blocks, level it in both directions with a long level, shim under the piers as needed and fasten it down before anything else goes on.

Notch the top of every 93" post before you stand any of them up — cutting a notch on a post already fastened to the deck means working overhead with a jigsaw, and the notches are what the roof frame nests into. Lay out each notch with a square and a pencil on all faces so the cut stays true through the 3-1/2" thickness, saw the cheeks with the circular saw set to depth and clean out the waste with the jigsaw, then test-fit an offcut of 4x4 in the notch. Stand the posts on the floor frame and fasten them with the Simpson Strong-Tie post connectors, or with 5" lag screws driven up from underneath the framing, or with pocket holes on the two inside faces — what you must not do is drive screws down through end grain, which has no holding power and is the whole reason the hardware is specified. Plumb every post in both directions and brace it temporarily with scrap before you move on; the roof frame will lock them, but only if they are plumb when it lands.

The gable frame is built from the four 58-1/4" long-side and four 37-1/2" short-side 4x4 rafters, each with one end cut at 45° and each length measured to its longest point. Notch both mating pieces at the peak so they nest into each other rather than butting — a lapped 4x4 peak is what gives this roof its strength without extra hardware, and notching only one side halves the joint. Cut the notches with the circular saw and jigsaw the same way you cut the post notches, dry-fit each pair on the ground and mark the mating faces before you take them apart. Assemble the gables on the ground where you can work flat, then lift them onto the notched post tops and drive 3" exterior screws from both the inside and outside faces of every lap. Add the two 77-1/2" header beams and the three 70-1/2" cross beams to tie the frame together, checking the whole roof for square against the deck below as you go.

Read this before you start, because the decking does not reach: thirteen 5-1/2" boards with twelve 1/2" gaps between them cover 77-1/2", against a floor frame whose every member is 89" long — 11-1/2" short, which is two more 2x6 than the cut list allows. All sixteen declared 2x6 are already spoken for by the thirteen decking boards and the three roof cross braces, so buy the extra two now or you will stop with a strip of bare joists. Lay the 96" boards across the 89" frame so they overhang about 3-1/2" at each end, and set that overhang equal on both sides before you fasten the first board. Use a 1/2" scrap as a spacer between boards so the gaps stay even and water drains, and drive two 3" exterior screws through each board into every joist it crosses, keeping the screws in line down the deck. Work from one edge to the other and check your run against the frame every few boards; a 1/8" drift per board becomes an inch by the far side.

Set the two 89" 2x8 ridge beams and the seven 89" 2x4 rafters onto the 4x4 gable frame, attaching them with pocket holes and the 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws driven from faces that will be hidden from below. Space the seven rafters evenly along the run and mark both bearing points from one layout so a rafter cannot end up skewed — the plan does not give a spacing, so set it out on the ridge first and transfer the marks. Check the roof for square by measuring diagonals across the rafter field before the last screws go tight. Then sand any rough edges and splinters within reach, and apply a finish rated for permanent outdoor exposure to every face you can get at, paying particular attention to end grain and to the cut faces of every notch — those are the places water gets in, and the ground-contact rating on the stock does nothing for a cut you made yourself.

flags: THE CUT LIST DOES NOT CLOSE, and the rewrite says so rather than inventing a joint. There are THREE 15" frame long sides where a rectangle needs two. Inside the frame nothing measures 15": the clear span between the two short sides is 13-1/2" and between the two long sides is 14-1/2", so the third piece fits neither opening. The source does not say what it is for.

The frame's reading is settled by the top's own overhang arithmetic, not by the box-geometry solver (which prints nothing for this plan). Long sides full / short sides between gives a 15" x 16" frame (14-1/2 + 3/4 + 3/4 = 16), and the top is 18-1/2" long by 17-1/2" wide (five 3-1/2" boards) — 18-1/2 less 16 and 17-1/2 less 15 are both 2-1/2", an even 1-1/4" all round, which is what the description says the top should do. The opposite reading gives a 16-1/2" x 14-1/2" frame and an uneven 1" / 1-1/2" overhang. The rewrite uses the even-overhang reading and states the evidence.

Consequence of that reading: the 18-1/2" tabletop boards run ACROSS the frame's 16" axis, and the five boards laid tight make up the 17-1/2" along the 15" axis. Spacing the boards apart would widen the top past 17-1/2" and destroy the even overhang; the rewrite says to lay them tight.
Exactly where each of the four legs registers on the outside of the frame is not specified in the source. The rewrite gives the flush-top requirement and the height arithmetic and stops.
Verified fine: nine 1x3 pieces totalling 139" fit two 8 ft boards; five 18-1/2" tabletop boards fit one 8 ft 1x4; finished height is 16-1/4" leg plus 3/4" top = 17".

ops.body: Build the frame from two 15" long sides and two 14-1/2" short sides, with the long sides running the full length and the short sides fitting between them — 14-1/2" plus 3/4" of stock at each end makes the frame 15" by 16" outside, which is the only reading that gives the tabletop the even overhang the plan calls for. Lay the four boards flat, countersink two pilot holes at each corner through the face of the long side into the end of the short side, and drive 1-1/4" exterior deck screws with glue on the joint. Measure both diagonals and adjust until they match before the last screws go tight; a frame out of square shows up as an uneven overhang the moment the top goes on. One thing the plan does not settle: it gives THREE 15" long sides where the rectangle uses two, and nothing inside the frame measures 15" — the clear openings are 13-1/2" and 14-1/2" — so set the third piece aside rather than forcing it in as a centre rail.

The four 16-1/4" legs go on the OUTSIDE faces of the frame rather than as square corner posts inside it — that is what makes this the faster of the two Adirondack companion tables and why no pocket-hole jig is needed. Stand the frame upside down on a flat surface and clamp each leg to a corner with its top end flush with the top edge of the frame; flush is the requirement, because the tabletop has to bear on the frame and the legs at the same time. Countersink and drive two 1-1/4" exterior deck screws through each leg into the frame member it covers, with glue between the faces. With the legs flush at the top, the finished table stands 17" tall: 16-1/4" of leg plus the 3/4" tabletop. Stand it up and check it doesn't rock before the glue sets.

The five 18-1/2" tabletop boards run across the frame's 16" direction, and laid tight edge to edge their five 3-1/2" widths make up 17-1/2" along the 15" direction. That is what produces the even 1-1/4" overhang on all four sides — 18-1/2 less 16 and 17-1/2 less 15 are both 2-1/2" — so lay the boards tight and do not space them out; a gap between boards widens the top and throws the overhang off. Dry-fit all five on the frame first and mark the overhang at each corner before drilling anything, exactly as the description says, because getting that border even matters more to how the finished table looks than any joint underneath. Predrill and countersink, then drive two 1-1/4" exterior deck screws through each board into each frame member it crosses.

Ease every edge and corner first — the top's four overhanging edges, the leg bottoms and the exposed end grain of the tabletop boards — so nothing catches a leg or an arm on the patio. Apply the exterior penetrating stain or paint to all faces including the undersides and the leg ends, since end grain sitting on a damp deck is where an outdoor table starts to fail. Two thin coats beat one heavy one; let each dry fully before the table goes out.
materials: Exterior penetrating wood stain or paint

baseStepTitles: Cross and join each leg set
Attach the side and back aprons
flags: THE SYSTEMIC GAP: this plan had NO step that cuts the parts at all — it opened on a dry-fit of pieces nobody had been told to make. One inserted at index 0, enumerated from the cut list.

Angled-cut arithmetic, derived and stated in the cut step: across a 2-1/2" wide 1x3 a 35° cut runs 2-1/2 x tan 35 = about 1-3/4" from long point to short point, and a 20° cut about 15/16". This is NOT the board thickness — do not let anyone re-write it as such.

The drawer box parts contradict each other and the rewrite says so instead of picking a side: the sides are 15-1/4" and the front/back 12" (a box roughly 12" to 13-1/2" wide depending on which member runs full), but the drawer bottom is listed at 16" x 44" and the drawer face at 44". The description's own rule — size the drawer to the actual opening, 1" narrower — is the only usable instruction, so the rewrite routes the builder there and tells them to cut the bottom to the box they actually built.
The four 15-1/4" drawer sides stack two layers per side EDGE ON EDGE, giving a 5" deep box (2-1/2 + 2-1/2). Face to face would give a 1-1/2" thick side and add no depth at all.
The height at which the bottom stretcher meets the legs is not specified in the source; the rewrite gives its 45" span and says the height is not fixed.

The desktop panel is bought at 24" x 48", which is exactly its cut-list size, so it needs no cutting — useful, because the tool list has no panel saw. The 16" x 44" 1/4" drawer bottom does need cutting from the quarter sheet.

Lumber verified sufficient, no shortfall: the 1x3 parts total 442-3/4" and pack into five of the six declared 8 ft boards; the two 24" side aprons and two 45" aprons total 138" out of the single 12 ft 1x4.

step.title: Cut all the parts
step.body: From the 1x3, cut two long leg pieces at 35-3/4" and two shorter full leg pieces at 31-3/8", all four with both ends at 35° off square and parallel to each other; four leg bottom fillers at 17-1/2", two leg top fillers at 14" and two notch supports at 18-1/4", each of those six with one end at 20° and the other at 35°. Also from the 1x3 cut the 45" bottom stretcher, four drawer sides at 15-1/4", two drawer front/back at 12" and the 44" drawer face. From the 12 ft 1x4 cut two side aprons at 24" with both ends at 35°, and two aprons at 45" — the front one is optional and gets skipped if you are building the drawer version. Every angled length above is a longest-point measurement, so mark the long point and let the short point fall where the blade puts it: across a 2-1/2" wide 1x3 a 35° cut runs about 1-3/4" from long point to short point and a 20° cut about 15/16", which is far more than most people expect and is exactly how a leg piece ends up short. The desktop panel is bought at 24" x 48" and needs no cutting; cut the 16" x 44" drawer bottom from the 1/4" plywood or hardboard. The 1x3 parts come to 442-3/4" and fill five of the six 8 ft boards with a 1/8" kerf, and the 1x4 parts to 138" of the 12 ft board, so you have one spare 1x3 — keep it, because a mis-mirrored leg piece is the likeliest mistake on this build. Label every piece as it comes off the saw and mark which face is up.

ops.body: Lay out all the leg pieces for both leg sets on the floor before gluing anything — you are building two mirror-image X assemblies, each from one 35-3/4" long piece and one 31-3/8" short piece plus 17-1/2" bottom fillers, a 14" top filler and an 18-1/4" notch support. Because every length is a longest-point measurement on an angled cut, a piece that looks right end-for-end can still be the mirror of the one you need, and that is only obvious with the whole set laid out. Arrange both X's side by side in the orientation they will finish in, and confirm each one measures 23-1/8" across the bottom spread — that is the number both leg sets must end up sharing, and it is far cheaper to find a mismatched angle now than after the glue is on.

Glue and nail a 14" leg top filler to the top of each long leg piece, face to face, exactly as you laminated the bottom fillers — the edges must line up flush, because that flush line is what makes the finished leg read as one solid piece instead of a stack. Its 20° end mates against the crossing short leg and its 35° end lands at the top where the apron and desktop will sit, so dry-fit it against the assembled X and check the orientation before a nail goes in; there is no forgiving way to pull a glued lamination apart. Use 1-1/4" finish nails and wood glue, and clamp or weight it while it sets. Do both leg sets and then sight across them to confirm the two top ends sit in the same plane — they carry the desktop between them.

The two 24" side aprons have both ends cut at 35° so they land flat against the sloped inside faces of the leg assemblies — set them to the inside of each X, not across its outer face. Attach them with glue and 2" finish nails, or drill 3/4" pocket holes and use the 1-1/4" pocket screws if you have the jig; the 1-1/4" length is what suits 3/4" stock, and a longer screw will break out the far face. Then stand both leg sets up and run one 45" apron across the back between them, holding it in place and marking rather than measuring twice — the desktop above is 48" long, so the aprons deliberately sit in from the ends. If you are building the drawerless version, add the second 45" apron across the front in the same way now; if you are building the drawer, leave the front open for it.

The bottom stretcher is a 45" 1x3 spanning between the two leg assemblies — the same 45" span as the back apron, so it registers off the same faces. Fasten it the same way, with glue and 2" finish nails or 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws. This piece is what stops the two X's from racking, so with it clamped and before it is fastened, stand the desk on a flat floor, check it does not rock, and confirm both leg sets are still square to the aprons. The source does not fix the stretcher's height on the legs, so pick a height that looks right against both X's, mark it on each leg from the floor rather than from the apron, and keep it level.

Measure your actual drawer opening before cutting anything, because the drawer parts in the cut list do not agree with each other: the sides are 15-1/4" and the front/back 12", which makes a box roughly 12" to 13-1/2" wide, while the drawer bottom is listed at 16" x 44" and the face at 44". The description's rule is the one to follow — build the box 1" narrower than the real opening, and cut the bottom to the box you actually built rather than to the listed figures. Stack the drawer sides two layers per side EDGE ON EDGE, so 2-1/2" plus 2-1/2" gives a 5" deep box; laminating them face to face would only give you a 1-1/2" thick side and no extra depth at all. Assemble the box with glue and 1-1/4" finish nails, add the plywood or hardboard bottom, and check it is square across the diagonals before the glue sets.

Attach the drawer slide members to the bottom side corners of the drawer box and the matching cabinet members to the inside of the desk, following your slide manufacturer's instructions — the 16" Euro slides want both members level and parallel, so mark their height off the desktop on both sides from one measurement. When closed, the drawer should sit slightly inset on the desk front; that is the intended campaign-style look, not a fit problem to chase.

If you have a pocket-hole jig, drill 3/4" pocket holes in the aprons before you attach the aprons and attach the desktop through them with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. Otherwise, nail and glue the desktop down with 2" finish nails.
